abstract: Members of the solute carrier organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) family are essential for the transport of a diverse array of amphipathic organic anions, ranging from endogenous metabolites like bile salts and steroids to critical pharmaceuticals such as statins and anticancer agents. Among these, OATP1B1 is fundamental to hepatic clearance, leading regulatory bodies like the FDA and EMA to mandate in vitro drug–drug interaction (DDI) testing for this transporter. Despite its physiological and clinical significance, the structural architecture and precise transport mechanisms of OATPs have remained poorly understood. In this study, we present cryo-electron microscopy structures of human OATP1B1 in complex with representative substrates—including the metabolites bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate, the antiviral drug simeprevir, and the fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein—captured in both outward- and inward-open conformations. These structures identify distinct major and minor substrate-binding pockets and delineate the conformational transitions required for transport. By integrating these structural findings with mutagenesis assays and molecular dynamics simulations, our work provides a comprehensive mechanistic framework for OATP1B1 function. These insights establish a structural basis for predicting DDIs and significantly advance our fundamental understanding of the OATP family.
